blocks.body: Reason: because / since / as shows you how to explain cause and notice the small differences in force and style between these words.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"When we explain reason, we often use because, since, and as. All three words connect a cause with a result, but they sound slightly different.","rules":[{"label":"Because = strongest cause","color":"emerald","text":"Because usually gives the clearest and strongest explanation of the cause.","example":"We stayed inside because it was snowing."},{"label":"Since / as = softer background","color":"blue","text":"Since and as often sound softer or more formal, as if the reason is already understood from the context.","example":"Since it was late, we took a taxi."},{"label":"Clause after the linker","color":"amber","text":"After these words, we use a full clause, not just a noun or gerund.","example":"because she was tired / since they had no time"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. The main meaning
blocks.body:
All three words answer the question why?

  • because - the most direct explanation of the cause.
  • since - the reason as shared background or logical basis.
  • as - similar to since, often slightly more neutral or written.

In everyday conversation, because is often the most natural choice when the reason really matters.

blocks.heading: 2. The usual pattern
blocks.body:
• Main clause + because / since / as + clause
    I left early because I felt ill.
  • Because / Since / As + clause, main clause
    Since the shop was closed, we went home.

After because / since / as, we need a full clause with a subject and a verb: because he was late, not because of he was late.

blocks.heading: 3. Examples and shades of meaning
blocks.body:
• She stayed home because she had a fever. - a clear, strong reason.
  • Since everyone was ready, we started the meeting. - the cause is presented as background logic.
  • As it was getting dark, we decided to turn back. - a calm, descriptive tone.

Because is often best when you are answering a direct Why? question.

blocks.heading: 4. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: using only a noun after because: because the rain. Here you need because it was raining or a different structure with because of.
  • Mistake: mixing up since as a reason linker and since as a time word.
  • Mistake: using as where the reason needs to sound very clear. In that case, because is often stronger.

blocks.heading: 5. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• Because = the most direct cause.
  • Since / as = softer or slightly more formal background cause.
  • After these words, use a clause, not just a noun.
  • If you are directly explaining why something happened, because is usually the safest choice.
